request.question: Robert King (born 1959) is an American film and television writer and producer, married to Michelle King, who is also his writing partner, The couple created The Good Fight, an American legal and political drama television series produced, for CBS's streaming service, called what?

sentences: Michelle King (née Stern; born 1958) is an American television writer and producer.
She is married to Robert King, who is also her writing partner.
The couple created the legal drama series "The Good Wife", which earned them a Writers Guild of America Award.
They have also created the comedy-drama "BrainDead".
"House of Cards" is an American political drama web television series created by Beau Willimon for Netflix.
The first season, based on the BBC miniseries of the same name and the series of books by Michael Dobbs, premiered exclusively via Netflix's web streaming service on February 1, 2013.

David W. Zucker is President of Television for Scott Free, and Executive Producer of the landmark series adaptation of Philip K. Dick’s classic “The Man in the High Castle” for Amazon, esteemed Civil War medical drama “Mercy Street” on PBS, the forthcoming first season of the Amazon half-hour action-comedy starring Jean-Claude Van Damme as “Jean-Claude Van Johnson,” the AMC anthological psychological thriller “The Terror,” “The Good Fight," the widely acclaimed CBS all-access spin-off of “The Good Wife,” and “Killing Reagan,” the fourth in the acclaimed National Geographic channel series of “Killing” telefilms (which include “Killing Jesus” and “Killing Kennedy,” both Emmy-nominated, plus the docudrama “Killing Lincoln”).

During his tenure, Scott Free has produced a steady stream of highly acclaimed programs including the Emmy and Golden Globe-nominated, Peabody-winning drama, “The Good Wife,” which recently concluded its 7th and final season on CBS, as well as “Numb3rs,” which ran for six successful seasons on the same network.

Notable long form programs include “Klondike” for Discovery, the Emmy and Golden Globe-nominated “The Pillars of the Earth” for Starz, “World Without End” for Reelz, Emmy-nominated “Into the Storm” for HBO, Emmy-nominated “The Andromeda Strain” and “Coma” for A&E, the Golden Globe-nominated “The Company” at TNT and a feature-length digital series for X-Box/Microsoft, “Halo: Nightfall.”
Acclaimed non-fiction programs include “Crimes of the Century” and the Emmy-winning documentary special “Gettysburg” for History Channel.

Previously, Zucker worked as a Story Editor on the CBS series, Judging Amy, wrote pilot scripts for CBS and ABC, as well as various stage plays, and served as VP of Drama Series for CBS in addition to VP of Drama Series & Current Programs at Warner Bros.
TV.
The Good Wife is an American legal and political drama television series that aired on CBS from September 22, 2009, to May 8, 2016.

The series focuses on Alicia Florrick, the wife of the Cook County State's Attorney, who returns to her career in law after the events of a public sex and political corruption scandal involving her husband.

The series, created by Robert and Michelle King, stars Julianna Margulies, Josh Charles, Christine Baranski, Matt Czuchry, Archie Panjabi, and Alan Cumming, and features Chris Noth in a recurring role.
The executive producers are Ridley Scott, Charles McDougall, and David W. Zucker.
"The Good Wife" is a heavily serialized show featuring several story arcs that carry over several episodes, as well as stand-alone procedural storylines that are concluded by the end of each episode.
The serial plots have been especially showcased in its highly praised fifth season.
This is a rarity among "The Good Wife"' s broadcaster CBS, as most of its shows are procedural.
Cheryl Heuton is an American television writer and producer.
Along with her husband and writing partner Nicolas Falacci, she co-created the television series "Numb3rs" (2005).
The couple created the show, a mathematics-centered departure from standard-fare Hollywood programming, to combat anti-intellectualism.
Falacci and Heuton were awarded the Carl Sagan Award for Public Understanding of Science in 2005 and, with "Numb3rs", the National Science Board′s Public Service Award in 2007.
Heuton and Falacci also co-wrote the TV movie "The Arrangement" (2013), an adaptation of Elmore Leonard’s story “When the Women Come Out to Dance.”
The Good Wife is a legal drama television series created by Robert King and Michelle King, which premiered on CBS on September 22, 2009.
The show tells the story of Alicia Florrick (Julianna Margulies), whose husband Peter (Chris Noth) has been jailed following a very public sex and corruption scandal.

She returns to her old job as a defense attorney under Will Gardner and Diane Lockhart (Josh Charles and Christine Baranski) to rebuild her reputation and provide for her two children, Grace and Zach (Makenzie Vega and Graham Phillips).
Robert King (born 1959) is an American film and television writer and producer.
He is married to Michelle King, who is also his writing partner.
The couple created the comedy-drama "BrainDead"; the legal drama series "The Good Wife", which earned them a Writers Guild of America Award; and "The Good Fight."
Barbara Hall (born July 17, 1960 in Chatham, Virginia) is an American television writer, producer, novelist and singer-songwriter.
She is known for creating and producing the CBS legal drama "Judging Amy" and the CBS fantasy family drama "Joan of Arcadia".
She served as co-executive producer on the Showtime political thriller "Homeland" and is the creator and showrunner of the CBS political drama "Madam Secretary", which premiered in the fall of 2014.
The Good Fight is an American legal and political drama television series produced for CBS's streaming service CBS All Access.
It is CBS All Access' first original scripted series.
The series is the sequel to "The Good Wife" which was created by Robert King and Michelle King.
The first season contains 10 episodes, and it premiered on February 19, 2017, with the first episode airing on CBS and the latter nine episodes airing on CBS All Access.
The series was initially planned to air in May 2017, but was moved up after production delays forced CBS to postpone the premiere of the new series "".
In Justice is an American television police procedural created by Michelle King and Robert King.
The series began airing on Sunday, January 1, 2006 on ABC as a midseason replacement and assumed its regular night and time on Friday, January 6, 2006 at 9 p.m. EST.
It was cancelled after its 13-episode run on March 31, 2006.
The series was simulcast in Canada on CTV.
In the UK "In Justice" was shown on UKTV Gold beginning September 17, 2006 and was later repeated on ABC1 in 2007.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

The couple created the comedy-drama "BrainDead"; the legal drama series "The Good Wife", which earned them a Writers Guild of America Award; and "The Good Fight."

Michelle King | Michelle King (née Stern; born 1958) is an American television writer and producer.  She is married to Robert King, who is also her writing partner.  The couple created the legal drama series "The Good Wife", which earned them a Writers Guild of America Award.  They have also created the comedy-drama "BrainDead".

The Good Fight | The Good Fight is an American legal and political drama television series produced for CBS's streaming service CBS All Access.  It is CBS All Access' first original scripted series.  The series is the sequel to "The Good Wife" which was created by Robert King and Michelle King.  The first season contains 10 episodes, and it premiered on February 19, 2017, with the first episode airing on CBS and the latter nine episodes airing on CBS All Access.  The series was initially planned to air in May 2017, but was moved up after production delays forced CBS to postpone the premiere of the new series "".

David Kohan | David Sanford Kohan (born April 16, 1964) is an American television producer and writer.  After writing for "The Wonder Years" and "The Dennis Miller Show", Kohan co-created and produced "Will & Grace", "Boston Common", "Good Morning, Miami", "Twins" and "Four Kings" with Max Mutchnick.  Kohan has won an Emmy and a People's Choice Award.  He has been nominated for a Golden Globe Award.  He and his business partner Max Mutchnick worked on a half-hour comedy series for CBS called "Partners".

His television writing credits include the NBC J.J. Abrams series ""Undercovers"", the critically acclaimed ABC Family cop/family drama series, "Lincoln Heights", and the CBS cop drama ""The District"". The series focuses on Alicia Florrick, the wife of the Cook County State's Attorney, who returns to her career in law after the events of a public sex and political corruption scandal involving her husband.  The series, created by Robert and Michelle King, stars Julianna Margulies, Josh Charles, Christine Baranski, Matt Czuchry, Archie Panjabi, and Alan Cumming, and features Chris Noth in a recurring role.  The executive producers are Ridley Scott, Charles McDougall, and David W. Zucker.  "The Good Wife" is a heavily serialized show featuring several story arcs that carry over several episodes, as well as stand-alone procedural storylines that are concluded by the end of each episode.  The serial plots have been especially showcased in its highly praised fifth season.  This is a rarity among "The Good Wife"' s broadcaster CBS, as most of its shows are procedural.

Robert Funaro | Robert Funaro (born January 23, 1959) is an American film and television actor.  Funaro is best known for the role of Eugene Pontecorvo in the Emmy Award-winning television series "The Sopranos".  His last episode on "The Sopranos", "Members Only," in which he played a major role, won an Emmy award for Best Writing in a Drama Series in 2006 for producer/writer Terence Winter.  He also appeared in the 2007 film "American Gangster" as Detective McCann and in 2012 appeared in "Whistle Blower" the 10th episode of the 2nd season of the CBS show "Blue Bloods" as NYPD Captain Browne.

Ambiguity / missing facts: none for the asked item (the streaming-service name is explicitly given). Bridge entity: "The Good Fight" (already present).

It is CBS All Access' first original scripted series.  The series is the sequel to "The Good Wife" which was created by Robert King and Michelle King.  The first season contains 10 episodes, and it premiered on February 19, 2017, with the first episode airing on CBS and the latter nine episodes airing on CBS All Access.  The series was initially planned to air in May 2017, but was moved up after production delays forced CBS to postpone the premiere of the new series "".

UFC Fight Pass | UFC Fight Pass is a subscription-based video streaming service owned by the Ultimate Fighting Championship.  The UFC Fight Pass consists of both a 24-hour linear streaming channel and on-demand programming from UFC's library.  UFC on-demand content launched for Xbox 360 on December 20, 2011.  Subscribers are able to view pay-per-view events in high definition, connect with friends to predict fight results, and have the ability to compare fighter statistics and records.  The UFC Fight Pass application was also planned for PlayStation 4 in early 2015.

Faith (name) | Faith is an English feminine given name derived from the word "faith."  It became popularized when the Puritans began using it as a virtue name during the 17th century.  Puritans also used Faith as part of longer phrase names, such as Be-faithful, Faithful, Faith-my-joy, and Fight-the-good-fight-of-faith.

Good Old Cause | The Good Old Cause was the name given, retrospectively, by the soldiers of the New Model Army, to the complex of reasons that motivated their fight on behalf of the Parliament of England.

Titan Fighting Championships | Titan Fighting Championship (Titan FC) is an American mixed martial arts promotion based out of Pompano Beach, FL.  Their shows were originally run in and near Kansas City and have since expanded to include venues all over North America and eventually, international locations.  Since July 2015, Titan FC events are broadcast on the UFC online streaming service UFC Fight Pass, which also showcases a library of the organization's previous events.
